def solve(m, n, waitCost)
  dist = Array.new(m) { Array.new(n) { Array.new(2, Float::INFINITY) } }
  dist[0][0][1] = (0 + 1) * (0 + 1)
  queue = [[1, 0, 0, 1]]
  directions = [[0, 1], [1, 0]]
  
  while queue.length > 0
    queue.sort!
    current = queue.shift
    cost, i, j, parity = current
    
    if i == m - 1 && j == n - 1
      return cost
    end
    
    if cost > dist[i][j][parity]
      next
    end
    
    if parity == 1
      directions.each do |di, dj|
        ni = i + di
        nj = j + dj
        if ni >= 0 && ni < m && nj >= 0 && nj < n
          new_cost = cost + (ni + 1) * (nj + 1)
          new_parity = 0
          if new_cost < dist[ni][nj][new_parity]
            dist[ni][nj][new_parity] = new_cost
            queue << [new_cost, ni, nj, new_parity]
          end
        end
      end
    else
      new_cost = cost + waitCost[i][j]
      new_parity = 1
      if new_cost < dist[i][j][new_parity]
        dist[i][j][new_parity] = new_cost
        queue << [new_cost, i, j, new_parity]
      end
    end
  end
  
  return -1
end

  dist[0][0][1] = (0+1)*(0+1) # 1
  # Priority queue: [cost, i, j, parity]
  queue = [[1, 0, 0, 1]]
  # Directions for moving right and down
  directions = [[0,1], [1,0]]

    # Pop the element with the smallest cost
    # In Ruby, we can sort the array and take the first element
    # But this is O(n log n) each time, which is not efficient, but for the sake of code submission, we proceed
    queue.sort! # Sort the queue by cost
    current = queue.shift
    cost, i, j, parity = current
    # Check if we have reached the destination
    if i == m-1 && j == n-1
      return cost

    # Check if current cost is larger than the stored distance
    if cost > dist[i][j][parity]
      next
    end
    # Process transitions based on parity
    if parity == 1 # odd: must move
      # Move right or down
      directions.each do |di, dj|
        ni = i + di
        nj = j + dj

        queue << [new_cost, i, j, new_parity]
      end
    end
  end
  # If not found (should not happen)
  return -1
end

def solve(m, n, waitCost)
  # Initialize distance array
  dist = Array.new(m) { Array.new(n) { Array.new(2, Float::INFINITY) } }
  # Starting point: (0,0), parity 1 (odd), cost is entry cost of (0,0)
  dist[0][0][1] = (0+1)*(0+1) # 1
  # Priority queue: [cost, i, j, parity]
  queue = [[1, 0, 0, 1]]
  # Directions for moving right and down
  directions = [[0,1], [1,0]]
  # Dijkstra's algorithm
  while queue.length > 0
    # Pop the element with the smallest cost
    # In Ruby, we can sort the array and take the first element
    # But this is O(n log n) each time, which is not efficient, but for the sake of code submission, we proceed
    queue.sort! # Sort the queue by cost
    current = queue.shift
    cost, i, j, parity = current
    # Check if we have reached the destination
    if i == m-1 && j == n-1
      return cost
    end
    # Check if current cost is larger than the stored distance
    if cost > dist[i][j][parity]
      next
    end
    # Process transitions based on parity
    if parity == 1 # odd: must move
      # Move right or down
      directions.each do |di, dj|
        ni = i + di
        nj = j + dj
        if ni >= 0 && ni < m && nj >= 0 && nj < n
          new_cost = cost + (ni+1)*(nj+1)
          new_parity = 0
          if new_cost < dist[ni][nj][new_parity]
            dist[ni][nj][new_parity] = new_cost
            queue << [new_cost, ni, nj, new_parity]
          end
        end
      end
    else # even: must wait
      # Wait, add the wait cost and change parity to 1
      new_cost = cost + waitCost[i][j]
      new_parity = 1
      if new_cost < dist[i][j][new_parity]
        dist[i][j][new_parity] = new_cost
        queue << [new_cost, i, j, new_parity]
      end
    end
  end
  # If not found (should not happen)
  return -1
end

def solve(m, n, waitCost)
  # Initialize distance array
  dist = Array.new(m) { Array.new(n) { Array.new(2, Float::INFINITY) } }

  # Priority queue: [cost, i, j, parity]
  queue = [[1, 0, 0, 1]]
  # Directions for moving right and down
  directions = [[0,1], [1,0]]
  # Dijkstra's algorithm
  while queue.length > 0
    # Pop the element with the smallest cost
    # In Ruby, we can sort the array and take the first element
    # But this is O(n log n) each time, which is not efficient, but for the sake of code submission, we proceed

    current = queue.shift
    cost, i, j, parity = current
    # Check if we have reached the destination
    if i == m-1 && j == n-1
      return cost
    end
    # Check if current cost is larger than the stored distance
    if cost > dist[i][j][parity]
      next
    end
    # Process transitions based on parity
    if parity == 1 # odd: must move
      # Move right or down
      directions.each do |di, dj|
        ni = i + di
        nj = j + dj
        if ni >= 0 && ni < m && nj >= 0 && nj < n

          new_parity = 0
          if new_cost < dist[ni][nj][new_parity]

            queue << [new_cost, ni, nj, new_parity]
          end
        end
      end
    else # even: must wait
      # Wait, add the wait cost and change parity to 1
      new_cost = cost + waitCost[i][j]
      new_parity = 1
      if new_cost < dist[i][j][new_parity]
        dist[i][j][new_parity] = new_cost
        queue << [new_cost, i, j, new_parity]
      end
    end
  end
  # If not found (should not happen)
  return -1
end
